chunk_summary: Instrumental swallowing studies (VFSS and FEES) are essential for evaluating airway safety and aspiration risk when bedside assessments are insufficient, especially in cases of recurrent pneumonia, neuromotor impairments, or structural anomalies.
learning_objectives: Understand the indications for VFSS and FEES in pediatric feeding and swallowing evaluations.
Differentiate between VFSS and FEES based on their advantages, limitations, and clinical applications.
Identify clinical scenarios requiring instrumental swallowing studies, such as recurrent respiratory symptoms or complex neuromotor impairments.
Learn how to coordinate VFSS/FEES studies with SLP and ENT services in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: VFSS provides dynamic imaging of oral and pharyngeal phases across multiple textures but involves radiation exposure.
FEES avoids radiation, visualizes pharyngeal anatomy, and can be repeated, but does not capture the actual swallow during white-out.
Indications for VFSS/FEES include recurrent pneumonia, unexplained wheeze, suspected silent aspiration, complex neuromotor impairments, and structural anomalies like cleft palate.
Studies should test compensatory strategies (e.g., pacing, texture changes) rather than solely confirming aspiration.
In India, availability of VFSS/FEES varies; prioritize studies for children with recurrent hospitalizations or unresponsive feeding interventions.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Instrumental swallowing studies are indicated when bedside evaluation cannot determine airway safety or when aspiration risk drives management decisions. (ASHA Practice Portal)
document_key_topics: Indications for VFSS and FEES in pediatric populations
Comparison of VFSS and FEES modalities
Clinical scenarios requiring instrumental swallowing studies
Coordination of VFSS/FEES in resource-limited settings
